text: Advanced RAG techniques go beyond the basic pipeline to improve retrieval quality and system reliability. Multi-query retrieval generates multiple variations of the original query and runs retrieval for each. Different phrasings capture different relevant documents. Results are merged and deduplicated before reranking. This improves recall significantly for ambiguous or complex queries. Hybrid sea

l significantly for ambiguous or complex queries. Hybrid search combines vector-based semantic search with traditional keyword search. Semantic search understands meaning but can miss exact terms. Keyword search finds exact matches but misses synonyms. Together they cover each other's blind spots and produce better recall and precision. Metadata filtering narrows the search space before similarity

etadata filtering narrows the search space before similarity search runs. By filtering on attributes like source, date, category, or department, the system reduces noise and improves both speed and accuracy. Agentic RAG replaces the fixed pipeline with a decision loop. An agent retrieves documents, evaluates whether they are sufficient, and decides whether to answer, refine the query, or try a dif

nd decides whether to answer, refine the query, or try a different approach. This handles complex and multi-part queries that fixed pipelines cannot. Graph RAG stores knowledge as a network of entities and relationships. Instead of retrieving isolated chunks, it traverses the graph to retrieve connected information. This is powerful for root cause analysis, knowledge management, and relationship q

oot cause analysis, knowledge management, and relationship queries. Production RAG systems require careful attention to latency, caching, monitoring, and evaluation. Caching responses for repeated queries reduces LLM calls and improves response time. Monitoring query latency, retrieval quality, and answer accuracy helps identify degradation early. Regular evaluation against a test dataset ensures

on early. Regular evaluation against a test dataset ensures the system maintains quality over time. Fine-tuning and RAG are complementary. RAG handles knowledge that changes or is private. Fine-tuning handles consistent behavior, tone, and output format. Most production systems use both.

RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation. It is a technique that improves LLM accuracy by retrieving relevant documents before generating answers. Instead of relying solely on training data, RAG fetches real, up-to-date information from a knowledge base and passes it as context to the LLM. The basic RAG pipeline works as follows: a user submits a query, the retriever searches the knowledge ba

ser submits a query, the retriever searches the knowledge base for relevant document chunks, those chunks are passed to the LLM as context, and the LLM generates a grounded answer based on the retrieved information. RAG reduces hallucinations because the model has real documents to reference rather than generating from memory. It also supports private and internal data that was never part of the m

ports private and internal data that was never part of the model's training set. Embeddings are central to RAG. An embedding model converts text into numerical vectors that capture semantic meaning. Similar text produces similar vectors, enabling semantic search that finds relevant documents even when exact words don't match. Vector databases store these embeddings and enable fast nearest-neighbor

ases store these embeddings and enable fast nearest-neighbor search at scale. When a query arrives, it is embedded and compared against stored vectors to find the most semantically similar chunks. Chunking is the process of splitting large documents into smaller pieces before embedding. Proper chunk size and overlap are critical for retrieval quality. Too large and embeddings capture too many topi

eval quality. Too large and embeddings capture too many topics. Too small and chunks lose context. Reranking is a second pass that re-scores retrieved documents for precision. After broad retrieval returns many candidates, a reranker selects the most genuinely relevant ones to pass to the LLM. Context construction assembles the reranked chunks into a clean, structured block that the LLM can reason

hunks into a clean, structured block that the LLM can reason over. It handles deduplication, ordering by relevance, and enforcing token limits. Prompting with retrieved knowledge requires explicit instructions. The LLM must be told to use only the provided context and to say it does not know when the answer is not present. This is the key guardrail against hallucination.
